speaker: interviewer
text: --and they that-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: --because some of them have been running short, so he said you can-- he said go on over three--

speaker: interviewer
text: I look at it this way. I know what I do. Like last week, I interviewed a woman and she was done in 40 minutes. So I feel like I-- I can consciously know, OK, so I can-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no so if she went 40 minutes, if somebody else goes over, it all kind of-- I think it will even itself out somewhere along the line.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they assign you a certain-- do they give you like eight tapes for each interview, or how does it work? Generally, the camera--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ready to slate? November 1, 1994. The survivor is Anna Fischer. The interviewer is Merle Goldberg, in the city of Los Angeles, in the United States, and the interview will be conducted in English. OK, we can begin now. First, if you could tell me your full name, including your maiden name, where you were born, and when.

speaker: interviewee
text: My full name was [? Hannah ?] [? Zimmer ?] Lipszyc. I was born on the 27th of September, 1920, in the city of Lodz--

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you're married name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My married name is Anna Fischer.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. If you could tell me a little bit about your family, and what life was like in Lodz, what growing up was like for you in Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: With-- with-- I-- I will tell you what was growing up for me, because, you know, there were-- as I say, I was born into a Hasidic-- I don't know if you know, maybe you heard about the [INAUDIBLE] called [INAUDIBLE], with one the [INAUDIBLE]. And it was a big-- and my-- my family-- my father's family-- was somehow related to them, and my cousin-- unfortunately, she's dying now, lives in Israel, who went to Palestine 1975.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, [INAUDIBLE] they got married. She's always telling me, you don't know what a [YIDDISH] you are. Well, you know what [YIDDISH] means? What do you say, it was somebody in a very special, aristocratic, Hasidic family. Because-- because [? Svasemesk ?], who was a great [INAUDIBLE] I don't know how many generations ago-- he was related to-- to my father, to my grandfather, to my great-grandfather, I suppose.

speaker: interviewee
text: Besides, I really didn't know my father's family so well, because they lived in Skierniewice. My father was born in Skierniewice. So my-- my grandfather was still alive. My grandmother died in the war in Skierniewice, because she was, in our eyes, she was a very old woman. I don't know if she was older then I am now, but, you know, of course, she was a very old woman. And-- and my father has one sister, [? Edka, ?] and she lived-- they all lived in Skierniewice. One of her daughters survived, [? Merta ?], who lives now in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as I say, it was a very-- I suppose, a Hasidic, for me, a very average home. We were not rich, and we were not starving. I suppose [INAUDIBLE]. You know when you grow up, you really don't pay so much attention. I mean, we were considered-- we were going to private schools, because my parents didn't want my brothers to be going to-- there was this cheder, which was also secular in Lodz, called the [? Lisatz. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And all the-- again, called, quote, unquote, 'aristocratic Hasidic kids.' The boys were-- were going there. And then there was my oldest sister, who went to a regular gymnasium. But it also was owned, I think, by [? Gashiniska ?] was the name, [? Gashiniska ?] [? Nicwanoa. ?] When I started kindergarten, I think maybe for two years kindergarten in the first class with all German, Polish, I started with the [? Gashiniska ?] but then Bais Yaakov, the regular Bais Yaakov school was open, and we all were transferred there. So-- and life was neither exciting, neither not exciting. We-- you know, the Jewish holidays, and we were supposedly-- you know, we were not allowed to go down and play in the yard. I don't know if you know those Polish yards now. We were there. It was just-- just like one big garbage pail, but it was kept. You know. We-- we did not have a house. We lived in an apartment, which was consisted of five rooms and a kitchen. That's how it was called. But we were a large family. And my grandmother, my mother's mother, who was-- my grandfather, I didn't know. He died in [? Zygorev. ?] So my grandmother-- my mother was an only child, so she lived with us. And actually, she was a-- well, as I say, I really was very, very fond of her. I didn't know my father's mother so well, and I also know that sometimes when she was coming to visit, there was always something, you know. She thought that, you know, her son wasn't-- that her daughter-in-law was not a perfect daughter-in-law. I remember my mother sometimes cried after my grandfather-- mother left. But my mother's mother was-- I mean, she was really-- she wonderful. You know. If you wanted to go to visit a friend, or invite somebody, you know, or go and make your lessons, you know, do your homework, and my mother would say, no, you do it first. And I would always run to my grandmother, and then she would always somehow finagle it out for us. She died in 1935, I remember that very well. She died Erev Yom Kippur. But she was very sick. She had high blood pressure and heart conditions, and everything else which comes with old age, especially at that time. And so then-- then I finished school in 1939, and then two months later-- there were-- you know, in a funny way, of course, things were going on in Germany. And-- and the Poles always said-- you probably know a little bit about Polish history. There was this Jozef Piłsudski, who was a great Polish hero. You know. Poland gained-- regained independence after the First World War. And-- he was actually well-disposed towards the Jews, so Piłsudski was considered wonderful to the Jews. After-- but it must have been quite a bit of anti-Semitism, anyway. And then later on, when you come to learn world history, and the world, after the-- after we left Poland you find out that Piłsudski was a-- was a dictator. He was. But then after he died, and there were I mean, there was always several anti-Semitic parties. [INAUDIBLE], party, and there was this other. I mean, there's-- this was a peasants' party. There were always very-- there were always anti-Semites, because [INAUDIBLE] the Jews, I mean, historically-- I'm not going to go through all this Polish history, but historically, of course, the Jews couldn't even-- or very few could even buy land and settle on the land. So when they were brought into Poland by this one Polish king in the fourteenth century-- 1348 or something-- they were bringing-- they were brought in an order to sort of establish towns, become tax collectors, become money lenders, because every king was constantly fighting wars or some other duels with some other kings.

speaker: interviewer
text: So their position was immediately such that the Poles where-- I mean, it was already a country, and it was at that time a feudal-- feudal system-- and the Jews became the ones who were selling them things. And they were appointed by the kings, or by those other [INAUDIBLE] or by the other dukes or by other aristocracy, the-- the tax collectors for them. So-- and-- and then-- I'm sure you know Shakespeare play, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what's it? that was his-- that was his position, and that was his downfall, because he-- he-- the Catholics don't-- I mean, they don't-- are not allowed to take, usually-- you probably know that, too-- if they-- if they lent them money. So this was left to the Jews, and then when like Shylock came back for his money, so that was his downfall. So were you experiencing--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, in-- in a funny way-- I was going-- I was going to a Jewish school. I didn't know any non-Jewish people, except for the-- some stories. You make-- and even then. And I think in the apartment house where we lived, there was maybe one or two non-Jewish families, and the concierge. So you knew about anti-Semitism, but until a certain point, you really did not-- you really didn't really personally feel it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I mean, I mean, the papers were full, and the Jewish press was full-- there were a lot of Jewish newspapers-- it was about-- there was constantly something going on in the parliament, you know, about the kosher slaughter that shouldn't be allowed, about, for instance, the stores were-- it was very, very strict, forbidden to sell anything on a Sunday. So if you went-- if you ran out of-- of milk-- you never did, because there was always somebody to bring the milk to the house-- but I mean, life was so primitive. But if you ran out of something and you went to the grocery store from, you know, from the back door, if a policeman caught you or caught the grocer who sell-- sold it to you, you were paying a high fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as I said, per-- until, the only time-- well, you know, our school had-- was a regular gymnasium then, but they did not have the right to-- to give you the [? baklava, ?] to give you the [? matora. ?] So we were five or six of us. One-- two of them are both girls also in this picture too, [? Shatya ?] and [? Shalayvan. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were-- we were supposed to take our exams in a gymnasium called [? Pinikap ?], which was a boy's gymnasium, and when we came there, this was-- and of course we got, you know, the instructions, they wanted to do-- and this was the only time that we-- that we-- because they came to us, one or two of the men, because this was a Polish [INAUDIBLE] gymnasium. I mean, there was no college education in any-- at any rate. So they said to us-- one said to me-- if you are going to help me-- whatever it was. Chemistry, I don't remember, or calculus. I don't think I remember any more. I wouldn't remember what I did anyway. So he said, I will help you with Polish literature. I got so offended, because I really-- we were very, very well prepared. Considering the fact that we were Jews, we were good students.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and then, when I wrote my-- you know, there was always a written test, and then they would come and do the oral test. When I came to the oral test-- and you don't know Polish literature, it wouldn't make any difference. It was [? Kashinski ?] writer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the professor said to me, if you were not a Jew, I would give you the highest grade, which was five-- you know, we were going from five to, all the way down to one. I mean, you started from one was very unsatisfactory, and five was the highest. But since you are a Jew, I can only give you a four. It was fine with me. But this was the only time when I-- you know, that he expected, because I am a Jew, I-- I cannot know-- feel-- the Polish, what, the soul, as Nazis used to say. You know, the [? Irish ?] soul, you cannot feel it. Or-- So that's-- that's about the story it is. You know, life was going on. And my older sister got married. I don't remember, it must have been in '32, '33. She was the only one married. And when the war broke out, my two oldest brothers above me, they were both engaged, one-- also to, you know, Hasidic girls. One from Lodz. They were supposed to be married in-- in December. Of course, they never did. And one was a girl from [? Benjein. ?] But of course, those marriages were never-- never took place. How many children were in your family, including you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were eight children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can you also tell me-- so, you didn't-- your world really didn't exist outside of the Jewish world in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very much. Apart-- except for the effect that somehow-- I don't know, maybe through the influence of my sister-- and because it's a big city, and I suppose, also, some, I don't know, intellectual curiosity. I was always interested, and movies, of course, were very popular. And we were even-- but this was because my older sister, her best friend, who was not of a very religious Jewish-- I mean, she was Jewish, but not religious-- and it was a very, what-- what you would call, you know, a very emancipated family. And she was a ballet teacher, and she insisted to give ballet lessons to me and to my younger sister. So we did, you know, and I just loved it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I'm saying, we were-- we were exposed more to also to secular cultural activities. And my mother was quite interested for some reason. And of course, then when you, I mean, you-- you get to be a teenager you read all kinds of literature, Polish literature. There was-- I did not know where-- how it come-- come about, how it came about, but I was interested in I don't know, you know, a little bit of music, a little bit of theater-- we were going-- Was there a large Yiddish theater in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] You know, there were-- there were Yiddish plays. I don't know, you've probably never heard, but when we first came here after the war, there was an actor who was an excellent actor. Leo Fuchs-- Leo Fuchs, you remember him? Him, I think, I saw once in the-- in the Yiddish theatre in Lodz. And there was a-- there were the Germans, as I remember also. There was the German actors who were coming to Poland because of-- of Nazis. And [? Granach ?] maybe was somebody. Some very famous Jewish actors and-- we would go to see, but those plays already were mostly dealing with what was going on Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but they were presented in Poland. That's why-- and then, of course, after [INAUDIBLE], after-- the-- residents, you know, the Jewish residents who were not citizens of Germany were, you know, were simply deported to, you know, to No Man's Land. And-- and I remember my oldest brother went down there because my mother has a cousin who lived in Halle in Germany. His son is still alive. He actually was-- he was [INAUDIBLE] Aliyah He went to Palestine. He wasn't home anymore when [INAUDIBLE] happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So quite a number of Jews came, too. And then of course, the Jewish community tried to-- the Jewish council, I mean, tried to-- tried to help along. But then we became much more aware. But still, you know, I don't know. We always were saying so foolishly-- we were saying, I remember, that our generation is the first generation that never lived through a war. And-- and I think, sometimes when we talked about it among us as friends, because there was the League of Nations, and what they did and what they didn't do for the Jews in Germany. So-- Now of course, you know much more of what was happening at that time. Your husband told us after the fact that you-- that you were sort of secretly engaged, or you were boyfriend and girlfriend. How did you come to meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we [? met ?] we met in Skierniewice. You know, because it wasn't even secretly engaged. It was we were going to get married after the war. I mean, he might have not been very acceptable to my parents, either. [LAUGHS] You know, I say-- [INAUDIBLE]. We met-- I don't know. This is another story. Lodz, you know, became part of-- of the German Reich. You understand what I mean, German Reich, the German nation, of the German country. So-- and even the name was changed to Litzmannstadt. You probably heard that too, I suppose, from some other survivors. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Lodz was, as long as I remember, I think one of the very first cities-- big cities that was-- that was told that there's going to be a ghetto. And if I remember correctly, seems to me that it was even towards the end of '39. I think I told you on the phone, this thing. Yeah, I'm-- I'm almost sure. And my aunt-- my father's-- in Skierniewice-- and if we showed you on the map, Skierniewice was part of the [? general ?] [? government, ?] it was part of-- I mean, of the Germans-- the Germans occupied it, but it was not incorporated. So-- and there was this Polish man-- I suppose he was one of those intermediaries between my parents. I-- I personally wasn't involved in this thing. I mean, nobody was asking my advice. But my-- my oldest brother and my other brother, my parents, my aunt and my cousins, all the sons of-- of my aunt, so that we should definitely not go to the ghetto. But I think there was a deadline set, which I think if I'm correct was the 30th of March, 1940. So after these communications through this Pole-- he was bringing, you know, correspondence from my-- from aunt and we were exchanging communications. That-- and then immediately, very many people from the-- Jewish people from Lodz started moving away if they could. If they had a son or a daughter, or an uncle or whoever lived in Warsaw, many of the people went to Warsaw, not knowing, you know, what will follow. And-- and some other places, and again from the small community around Lodz, the Germans began already deporting people to, you know, to-- create a large Jewish center, and those Jewish centers became those big ghettos. Lodz, Warsaw, Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the advice of my aunt was that we should definitely not-- not go to-- look for a place in the ghetto, because it was really-- it was so pathetic. I remember one day I met one of my teachers who was a married woman. She was a lovely person. And she was so-- she was rushing, and I-- I always asked how are you-- I mean, how are you managing? She said, I have to find a place. She had two small children. I have to find a place in the ghetto. This was a slummy part of most cities. So-- going back to us, so my aunt advised that little by little-- you know, my mother, my father, my little sister-- they should all start smuggling themselves to Skierniewice. They found us an apartment in Skierniewice, and there immediately developed a smuggling deal, you know, because there was an actual frontier between-- between Lodz and [INAUDIBLE] because of that incorporation. And that as long as the ghetto is not closed, as long as the deadline isn't there yet, two of us should remain in the apartment. Maybe things will change. See, one-- when I think of it now, that-- how did one know? So my oldest brother and I, and some other neighbors, their sons also, and daughters-- there was a-- you know, that group of young adults-- said that we should stay there as long as we can. Because-- so we did. Until, when really time was getting very short, so the decision was made, you know, by the-- and also, you wondered, what is going to happen to the furniture? What is going to happen-- not that we had, you know, anything so terribly valuable, but, you know, a household of so many--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so that-- that's-- that's how it happens. I remember that night when we were smuggling ourselves to [? Brejiniez ?]. We were maybe 25 people sleeping on the floor there in that-- You go through this frontier in the middle of the night. It was January, I think. It was winter. It was snowing. It was-- and, of course, you know, you went by horse and buggy, those things. And so that's how I happened to come also to-- to Skierniewice. And the first couple of months, it was really considered-- I look-- I'm looking back. It was just wonderful. We were free. We could-- I mean, there was no ghetto. I met-- of course, you know, my cousins were there, of my age also, younger-- a little older. So I-- we had company, and-- and it was family, and we had an apartment which was already arranged by-- This was-- then came Passover. And that's where this-- there was one friend of mine whom I then met-- [? Steinschneider ?] was the family's name-- in Skierniewice also, because her family also had some relatives there. So one evening, I think in her house, I met Benno. So, it was-- actually, my parents probably wouldn't be very happy. You know, I didn't date before. It was-- we were supposed to be Hasidic girls. You probably know that, or maybe you don't know that. It's difficult to imagine. You did not date.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but-- but-- that's what maybe he meant. We started, you know, seeing each other, and he was writing me little notes, and we were sometimes-- you know, I'm going to [INAUDIBLE], he was there too, and a few other young boys, and all the young girls. So that's how we-- that's how we met. And we never thought of getting married now. We were-- we were going to get married after the war. I'm sorry-- by asking you about Benno, but-- what we missed was what happened to your-- what happened when the Germans invaded Poland? We kind of skipped over that part of it. So, if you don't mind, can we go back and can you tell me what happened to-- in Lodz, and anything you might have witnessed once the Germans came into Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- well, one could say with one word, it was really bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: But could you elaborate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I-- I remember first of all, you know, every day, there was new rules and regulations promulgated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us what some of those were?

speaker: interviewee
text: What they were about was-- I mean, there was hardly any-- but-- but this maybe also has to do something with the war conditions-- there was no bread. There was no food. There was-- there was nothing. Unless you had something prepared before, there was nothing. I remember my aunt, that same fellow-- what was his name? Tsikovsky or whatever. I don't remember his name. He would bring us something from-- from Skierniewice. A chicken-- which was a kosher chicken, kosher slaughtered-- or a dozen of eggs. Eggs, we didn't see them anyway. And another thing-- it was really very, very dangerous for a-- and then with this bread. You know, they would announce that those bakeries are going to have bread. So either my sister or I-- and this was when the anti-Semitism came in. We would get up at 2 o'clock at night, you know, to get to the line, [INAUDIBLE] those bakeries that might have bread. And they started maybe selling at 6:00 in the morning, and the line was getting longer and longer and longer, and you were standing there. It was cold. And then, after all this, one of-- the-- the-- of course, Germans were watching you look. And-- and then suddenly, one of the Poles would went-- would go to this German and start, you know, pointing out who he thinks is a Jew the line. And they would throw you out of the line. You went home without the piece of bread. It was black. You know, it was like clay.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that is one thing. And secondly, my brothers, my father once-- you simply could not get out of the house, because they would be immediately grab you. And you never knew. Some, maybe they killed and they never came back. My family at that time, fortunately-- but they would grab you. They would take you to wash all the latrines, latrines-- Latrines.

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Latrines.

speaker: interviewee
text: Latrines. For eight hours maybe. And you would come back filthy and dirty, and sometimes bleeding because you didn't work fast enough. Another thing was, which I remember because it also pertained to-- to women, you went out-- so mostly the boys were staying home. And if anything, you wanted to get something, it was usually myself and my sister, because not everybody immediately knew that we were Jewish because we were young and blonde. And-- And there were those rules and regulations that you can only-- cannot walk on the sidewalk where the Germans are walking. You have to walk in the middle of the street. And there were those horses, and there was-- you know, it was-- and it was something which was coming so unexpectedly. You know, here you are a person, for better or for worse. I mean, they loved you or they didn't love you. Never thought of it. You know, you didn't wake up every morning and think, I am a Jew and he's a Pole.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then suddenly-- you know, and I remember every single day. And then, of course, they took away all the Jewish businesses. My father didn't have a big business, but he had this store, which was also taken over. I mean, it was-- what-- what do you call it in English? It was actually, it was locked up so that there was-- my father had [INAUDIBLE] with a relative, one young employee. So I remember a few nights, maybe after midnight, my father and this young man, and I think my oldest brother-- there was a back-- back door, which I don't know whether there was also the seal or there wasn't a seal-- anyway, through this back door, they-- they stole and-- and stole-- literally stole out some-- I think I told you this, but all the materials. You know, the cottons and-- and linens for sheets, for pillowcases, for beds. Because all those things you didn't buy ready made. Everything was-- those [INAUDIBLE] that the peasants were wearing, what have you. So this [? all ?] came in meters. And so, let's say if you managed to cut 20 meters, you grab it, you know, and you were fortunate nobody caught you, because it was the middle of the night, and you brought it down. And then, this Pole would take it to Skierniewice-- and that actually, the first-- was the first year for sure. Maybe even more.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived on it, because my cousins and my sister and myself, they knew all those Polish peasants because they were customers of my aunt's. My aunt-- my aunt, again, was a retailer-- she was one of my father's customers. She was-- you know, she was in retail, and my father was this wholesaler, [? jobber ?] or whatever you call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We need to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you we were getting very close.

words.text: you we were getting very close.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I'm talking too--
